Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after my part-time job, but it rarely worked out consistently. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes, which took forever. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until panic set in. With 14 online courses, it was hard to keep track of everything, so I just dealt with whatever seemed most urgent.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. I wanted to be one of those people with a consistent schedule, but between work and everything else, it just didn't happen. When assignments were due, I'd cram hard for a few days. During quieter periods, I'd drift and tell myself I'd catch up later. The anxiety of not having a routine actually made things worse, which is kind of ironic.
Q3: Usually it was because something was due soon or I got a notification reminder. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more than I'd like to admit. Occasionally a classmate would message me about something, and that would prompt me to go look. It wasn't really a proactive thing for me—more reactive, like putting out fires.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in daily, usually at the start of a new module when I felt motivated. But that never lasted more than a week or two before life got in the way. The routine never stuck because something always came up—work shifts, helping family, or just feeling too mentally drained to engage. I wish it had become automatic, but it didn't.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me since I learn better visually. I could pause, rewind, and really study the diagrams and examples. I watched a lot of those—probably over two hours worth total. The downloadable slides were good too because I could print them out and annotate them by hand. Discussion boards weren't really my thing, but sometimes seeing other students' questions helped clarify stuff I was confused about.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums except for required posts. It felt awkward posting there, and the anxiety of saying something wrong in front of everyone was real. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because there just wasn't time with everything else going on. Some of the optional practice quizzes I meant to do but never got around to. When you're stretched thin on resources and time, you triage.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Visual stuff like videos or interactive diagrams always felt more worthwhile than walls of text. Also, if something was quick and gave me a sense of accomplishment, that helped. With my anxiety levels, I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just spinning my wheels.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required stuff had to get done no matter what, so that's where my energy went. Optional things always got pushed to 'later' which usually meant never. There's this guilt about not doing them, but also relief that I didn't have to. The pressure of required work was already overwhelming enough without adding more to the pile.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything so I could study offline—my internet at home isn't always reliable, and I can't afford to waste data. I'd print slides at the library and study from those on the bus to work. I also used YouTube videos to supplement when the course material wasn't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical notes spread out on my bed, not in front of a screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd download a lecture video once and then watch it multiple times offline over several days. The LMS would only show one access, but I'd have reviewed that material three or four times. Same with PDF readings—I'd print them and highlight them, make flashcards, study with friends. None of that shows up anywhere. My actual engagement was way higher than my login frequency suggests.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. Download once, study repeatedly offline. My resources are limited, so I had to be strategic about when I used internet and for what. I'd grab everything I needed in one session at the library, then work through it at home over the next few days. It saved data and let me study during commutes and breaks at work.
Q12: There were definitely hard periods, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety was through the roof, and I'd feel paralyzed—like there was so much to do that I couldn't start anything. Work was demanding more hours, and I was exhausted. Some days I'd stare at the LMS homepage and just close it because I couldn't handle facing all the overdue stuff. The peer support group helped a bit during those times.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them. Competing responsibilities were constant—I had to prioritize paying rent over studying sometimes. The fatigue was real; some nights I'd log in, stare blankly at a video for ten minutes, and give up because nothing was sinking in.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but be completely zoned out thinking about other stuff. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing any of it. My body was there, my eyes were on the screen, but my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or upcoming shifts. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they'd show up as 'activity' in the system.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they stressed me out so much. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for everyone to judge was paralyzing. I also avoided starting big assignments until the last minute because the scope felt overwhelming. It's not that I didn't know they were important—I knew exactly how important they were, which somehow made the avoidance worse. The anxiety fed on itself.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd be relatively quiet until about 48 hours before something was due, then suddenly I'd be on there constantly, downloading everything, watching videos at 1.5x speed, frantically trying to piece things together. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could function. The adrenaline of a deadline was sometimes the only thing that could cut through the anxiety paralysis.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback was motivating and made me want to engage more with that subject. But critical feedback, even when constructive, sometimes made me avoid that area for a while because it felt like confirmation that I wasn't good enough. The peer support intervention helped me process feedback better—talking to other students who felt the same way normalized it a bit.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, which I needed. But with 14 courses, the structures often conflicted—multiple deadlines hitting at once across different platforms. The weekly format helped when I could keep up, but once I fell behind, the structure made it obvious how much I was struggling, which increased my anxiety.
Q19: The visual elements like video lectures and diagrams made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or buried in text-heavy pages. The discussion requirements felt forced and uncomfortable. Having everything downloadable was crucial given my situation. I wish there had been more flexibility in deadlines for students juggling work and limited resources—that would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my bad days—logging in repeatedly, clicking around, trying to engage but not being able to focus enough to actually produce work. High anxiety can make you check things obsessively without being productive. Or maybe they're gathering information and planning to work offline. Sometimes activity is just anxious energy, not actual learning or progress toward submissions.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. I got a Merit despite low engagement scores because a lot of my real studying happened offline. I'd download materials once and work with them extensively away from the LMS. My exam scores were decent because I knew the content—I just learned it through printed notes and diagrams at home, not through tracked online activity. The LMS numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long pages are open. They're good at tracking submission times and login frequency. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the mental state behind the activity. They can't tell if someone's actually learning or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture anxiety, financial stress, or the effort it takes just to show up some days.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments—drawing diagrams, explaining things to myself, making connections. It's not about how many times you log in; it's about whether the learning is actually happening in your head and whether you give a damn about understanding it.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It can't show the anxiety attacks before logging in, the hours spent studying from printed materials on buses, or the peer support conversations that kept me going. It doesn't capture that I was working part-time, dealing with limited resources, and still managing to learn the content. My engagement was real—it just didn't happen in ways the system could measure.
